excerpt_en: Hey, it's MaSU. I'm writing this before I've even gone to Kuala Lumpur. Here we go!! I'm writing this while waiting at the check-in counter at Haneda Airport. So, I'll arrive in Kuala Lumpur
body_en:
Hey, it's MaSU.

I'm writing this before I've even gone to Kuala Lumpur.

Here we go!!

I've arrived at Haneda Airport and I'm writing this while waiting at the check-in counter.

So, I'll arrive in Kuala Lumpur on the morning of the 6th.

And from the afternoon,

I have 2 Masu Photo shoots scheduled with local people [Malaysians].

As I wrote yesterday,

I only contacted them through Instagram,

we haven't met face to face,

and I don't even know if they'll actually show up.

#All communication was in English

#If neither of the two pairs show up, please laugh at me

And the accommodation is a super cheap guesthouse at around 1,000 yen per night.

The reviews and ratings for the guesthouse were high,

so I'm hoping for at least a level of quality that won't be uncomfortable.

I've already purchased the express train ticket from Kuala Lumpur airport to KL Sentral station (the equivalent of Tokyo Station), so that shouldn't be a problem.

I'll wander around the station area,

and check in during the afternoon.

Since I still don't know the geography of Kuala Lumpur, for the 2 scheduled shoots,

I set the meeting point at the entrance of the guesthouse.

So, what's going to happen?

Masu Photo Version Upgrade

This time, FOMUS

has launched an accessories brand.

It's called "FOMUS Parure."

https://fomusparure.official.ec

FOMUS masu cups have been used as communication tools

in various fields,

but Masu Photo had become something "only MaSU takes photos of," and it wasn't spreading much beyond my own shoots.

So, models (especially women)

could use accessories in their everyday life,

and I thought it would help people feel closer to FOMUS and masu.

Here are the FOMUS Masu Photo models.

Furthermore, I'll have the models we shoot with in Kuala Lumpur wear them too, and spread the word to the world.

In Japan, the masu accessories will strongly serve their "meaning" as a communication tool for people involved with FOMUS,

while overseas, we aim for them to also be used as "fashion."

That said, whether these masu accessories can become popular or not,

we need to actually have local people wear them and hear their feedback.

I'll make sure to include this feedback in this newsletter.

So,

regarding the "Masu Photo" aspect of this Kuala Lumpur expedition

to spread FOMUS,

this is a two-pronged approach combining

Masu Photo shoots and

masu accessories promotion.

More specifically,

I feel like we could get four or five things out of this.

Of course, this is just a hypothesis,

and we'll verify it through the on-site activities starting tomorrow.

So,

we're about to depart!

Off to Kuala Lumpur!!

Feel free to leave comments if you'd like!

[Day 1] Activities in Malaysia Begin

excerpt_en: So, I've arrived in Kuala Lumpur. The morning was free time, so I headed to the National Textile Museum, which opens at 9 AM. They exhibit traditional craft costumes passed down from India, and it strengthened my image of combining them with FOMUS's official
body_en:
So, I've arrived in Kuala Lumpur.

The morning was free time,

so I headed to the National Textile Museum, which opens at 9 AM.

They exhibit traditional craft costumes passed down from India, and it strengthened my vision of incorporating those into the costumes of FOMUS's official character "KUKU."

-> What is KUKU?

https://fomus.kuku-dao.com

Today I had a photo shoot and a meeting with someone in the afternoon,

so I spent the morning touring the tourist spots.

The Kuala Lumpur City Gallery,

Merdeka Square, the symbol of independence,

the library where overseas nomad workers gather,

Central Market with tons of souvenirs,

and the Masjid Jamek temple.

And so on.

The tourist spots were pretty close together, so I made it around to most of them.

#Of course with Masu-Game photos along the way

And then, the afternoon shoot.

Today's model was a woman named Rekha,

who also serves as a tourism ambassador for Kuala Lumpur.

I only contacted her 2-3 days ago.

I wasn't sure if she'd actually come,

but after I finished checking in at the guesthouse, I got a DM saying,

"I'm already at the entrance of your guesthouse!"

Since I was a first-timer in Kuala Lumpur,

I had asked her to come to my guesthouse.

I'd heard that many Malaysians tend to be relaxed about time, so I thought she might be about 30 minutes late.

But she was there,

10 minutes early!!

I was really grateful.

Actually, since I had some time before checking into the guesthouse,

I was walking around the area and found a stylish garden,

so we decided to go there.

She came by car,

and right away, I got a ride to the shooting location.

I never expected to be riding in a local's car on the very first day haha

Masu Photo Shoot Begins

Since this was a Masu Photo shoot,

we jumped right in with the masu in hand.

As expected of a local model,

she posed freely on her own.

*There are no specific pose instructions for Masu Photo

I've done countless Masu Photo shoots myself,

so I can nail it whether the subject is from overseas or not.

My English skills aren't great, honestly,

but I push through with my natural cheerfulness and communication skills haha

And then!!

As part of this promotion,

she wore the masu earrings from FOMUS's accessories brand "Parure"!!

If you look at the photos, you can tell,

they look absolutely amazing!!!!

I already knew they looked good on Japanese people,

but I was curious about how they'd look on someone from overseas, and I had nothing to worry about!

She really liked the masu earrings too,

and after the shoot,

she said, "Can I buy these right now?"

Normally, I'd want to just give them to her,

but since these were for promotion and I only brought two of each type, I couldn't hand them over,

but I let her know they'll be released in October.

The data isn't sufficient yet,

so I can't say for sure,

but I have a feeling that FOMUS masu accessories

could spread overseas.

If there's enough demand,

we could even set up an exclusive sales contract at a shop somewhere in Kuala Lumpur.

By the way, I haven't been involved at all

in making these masu accessories haha

The person making them is Yuri, the Kansai-based FOMUS model who has helped the most with photo shoots.

#FOMUS is about leveraging everyone's strengths

The story behind these accessories is for another time!

So,

the masu accessories & FOMUS Masu Photo shoot went off without a hitch!

Meeting a Friend

After the shoot,

we headed straight to Suria KLCC, a shopping center.

There, I met up with a friend from college.

He's a web designer,

and currently lives as a digital nomad overseas,

traveling around mainly in Asia.

He happened to be in Kuala Lumpur, so I met up with him.

Our work is completely different, and our lifestyles are different too,

but I thought it was pretty cool that we could meet up overseas like this.

Someday, I want to meet up with FOMUS members

overseas too!

#If FOMUS masu cups are all over the world, wherever you go you can connect through masu

So,

it didn't come together as neatly as I'd like,

but the first day in Kuala Lumpur ended successfully.

Tomorrow, I'll be visiting someone who runs a shop in Kuala Lumpur, introduced through a contact from Kyoto.

It's a rare opportunity to hear from someone doing business in Kuala Lumpur, so I'll study hard and share what I learn!!

[Day 2] What It Means to Do Business in Kuala Lumpur

title_en: [Day 2] What It Means to Do Business in Kuala Lumpur
excerpt_en: So, Day 2 in Kuala Lumpur. Today, through an introduction from a friend who runs a hotel in Kyoto, I visited a restaurant owner in Kuala Lumpur. It was "Matcha Hero" in the Tokyo Street area of Pavilion, a large shopping mall in Kuala Lumpur.
body_en:
So, Day 2 in Kuala Lumpur.

Today, through an introduction from a friend who runs a hotel in Kyoto,

I visited a restaurant owner in Kuala Lumpur.

It was "Matcha Hero" in the Tokyo Street area of "Pavilion," a large shopping mall in Kuala Lumpur.

I got to hear about doing business in Kuala Lumpur

in a one-on-one conversation with the owner.

From here on, this gets into some real talk,

so please don't share this with others.

Problems with Doing Business in Malaysia

There were tons of things I learned

from listening to the owner,

and among them,

I'll write about the ones that really made me go

"I see!!"

The first is a quality issue.

This is "common in the restaurant business,"

but compared to Japan, the quality of sourced ingredients is often poor.

Matcha Hero

serves matcha lattes, ice cream, and other items,

with the matcha sourced from Kyoto

and the milk from Malaysia.

The matcha comes packaged from Kyoto,

but the Malaysian milk that comes in paper cartons

is sometimes spoiled.

Apparently, they have to check each one individually.

I worked part-time at a cafe during college,

but I never once considered "the possibility that the milk we're using might be spoiled."

In that regard, Japan is definitely better.

Another issue is "human resources."

In Japan, you often hear that people are the most important asset, right?

When doing business in Malaysia,

you're required to hire locals first.

If hiring through online postings proves difficult,

you need to provide reasons to hire Japanese staff.

Matcha Hero

often hires Malaysians as part-time workers,

but perhaps due to cultural differences, many of them tend to be easygoing.

Not showing up on time for shifts is apparently normal,

and they come up with all sorts of reasons not to come in.

The owner has had a lot of struggles with this.

Now, they hire slightly more part-timers than needed,

and even if there's overlap, they make sure there's never a situation where no staff is available.

Japanese people are said to be very diligent,

and most are punctual,

but since the national character is different, people don't easily change.

I learned that when doing business in Kuala Lumpur,

you have to deal with things you'd never think about in Japan.

However, it's not all negatives --

there are plenty of advantages too.

One is the "low wages."

The hourly wage for locals working at a restaurant in a shopping mall

is roughly 10 RM.

That's about 300 yen.

Full-time is only about 100,000 yen.

That's very attractive!

*That said, even if you raise wages,

the national character doesn't change, so people who are late will still be late.

The person I spoke with today

is from a company expanding sales channels primarily through Japanese matcha,

and our "FOMUS" business is also

primarily about "masu," which is Japanese,

so the conversation turned to expanding Japanese content globally.

Matcha Hero

generates revenue from the physical store, of course,

but beyond in-store sales,

they also serve as a distributor for matcha to places like Thailand.

For the Kyoto matcha shop too,

having a distributor in Kuala Lumpur

is mutually beneficial.

In Kuala Lumpur,

there's another matcha brand,

but they reportedly rebrand and sell under their own name.

However, Matcha Hero

also sells the Kyoto matcha powder in their store

under the original matcha company's name,

which gives it high credibility for overseas customers,

and many people buy in bulk.

From this, I can see that

for Matcha Hero,

the front-end is the physical store selling matcha lattes and ice cream.

The back-end is the matcha powder sold in-store.

And then, the matcha distributorship.

That's the structure.

Hearing this,

if I were to apply this to FOMUS,

FOMUS masu would be the front-end.

*Maybe it's masu x food, or art, or accessories.

And then,

the back-end would be being a distributor for a masu maker [supply chain].

I started to think that way.

In reality, FOMUS often receives offers from people saying "We want to make our own masu!" and we pass them to the masu maker,

but when I think about it, that might be a wasted opportunity.

FOMUS has been promoting masu through various approaches,

and if we're going to take masu global, I thought that the one best positioned to serve as a distributor might be "FOMUS" right now.

By the way, talking about FOMUS as of September 2023,

the front-end is masu, events, and Masu Photo,

and the back-end is MaSU's consulting (AI and NFT).

*I don't publicize this much.

However, with this back-end model,

it doesn't work without MaSU being there,

so today's conversation was really educational for me personally.

Lastly,

the owner of Matcha Hero

got the opportunity for a Kuala Lumpur shopping mall location at age 31, opened at 33, and has been running it for 8 years.

As for why the shopping mall opportunity came along in the first place -- during his time as a company employee, he worked on shopping mall events in Japan and built connections with all sorts of people.

In other words, he worked his 20s fully as a company employee, earned recognition from various people, built connections, and when a big chance came in his 30s, he was ready to seize it.

Now in his 40s, he has two stores, has built a supply chain system with various countries,

and can even do his kids' school drop-offs and pick-ups.

Hearing stories like this,

as someone in my late 20s,

I was reminded once again how incredibly important it is to go all-in on what's right in front of you, build your physical and mental strength, and cherish your connections with people.

When a big opportunity comes in your 30s,

you need to be someone who can seize it.

At this point in time,

being able to talk about business with someone actively running a business overseas was a huge learning experience for me personally.

This isn't limited to business owners --

I think it's something that applies to everyone.

This article

ended up being very business-focused

and long and a bit scattered,

but that's how much there was to write about.

#If it's hard to follow, just ask ChatGPT to summarize it

#It should make things clearer

So, tomorrow I have another Masu Photo shoot scheduled.

I have no idea if they'll actually show up haha

#My last confirmation message was left on read

I'll enjoy even that uncertainty

and keep pushing forward with my activities!!

Thanks for your continued support!!

[Day 3] Japanese Content Is Strong

excerpt_en: Day 3 in Malaysia. In the morning, I had a shoot with a model based in Kuala Lumpur. This Masu Photo was decided just last night, so I was worried about whether she'd actually come, but we managed to do the shoot! The model was
body_en:
Day 3 in Malaysia.

In the morning,

I had a shoot with a model based in Kuala Lumpur.

This Masu Photo was decided just last night,

so I was worried about whether she'd actually come,

but we managed to do the shoot!

The model was a woman named Elaine,

a 20-year-old who does modeling.

This time, her mother came along too.

Her mother's relatives teach Japanese in Hong Kong, apparently.

It goes without saying,

but neither of them speaks Japanese,

so all communication was in English.

The great thing about Masu Photo is

that you don't need to use any complicated words.

During the time we're not shooting, while moving around,

we communicate by chatting in English.

I think what separates a good photographer from a bad one

shows in these moments -- if the conversation is lively when you're not shooting, you naturally get great photos.

#Breaking the ice within 10 seconds of meeting someone

#Skills honed during my days as a hotel worker

And so,

today again we did a photo shoot with the masu accessories.

The shoot itself was about 30 minutes,

but in just that time, both the model and her mother

said, "I want to buy these accessories!"

Perhaps because the concept of masu doesn't even exist for them,

there's an appeal in being able to wear "traditional Japanese craftsmanship" as fashion?

I'd like to gather more reasons to understand this better.

Japanese Content Is Strong

Now, on a different note,

I went to the Kinokuniya bookstore in Kuala Lumpur.

Japanese anime and manga

are content where Japan can win globally,

so I made sure to study up on that front too.

Perhaps because Kuala Lumpur has so many different ethnicities,

the English, Chinese, and Japanese editions

were each placed in separate booths.

What was interesting

was that the prices varied depending on the translation language.

For example,

Spy x Family was

English edition: 47.65 RM

Chinese: 20.9 RM

Japanese: 30.36 RM

Oshi no Ko was also

English edition: 64.22 RM

Chinese: 26.9 RM

Japanese: 37.48 RM

1 RM = 31 yen (as of 09/08/2023)

The English editions are overwhelmingly more expensive.

For Oshi no Ko,

the Japanese edition is 1,200 yen

while the English edition is 2,000 yen.

Shipping costs

and paper material costs may be factors,

but it's quite pricey, isn't it?

I wonder why there's such a big price difference haha

Since it's Kinokuniya,

the selection of Japanese manga and novels was incredibly rich.

There were even shelves dedicated to

the original comics of anime currently airing in real time,

which I thought was a setup made possible

by an era where anime can be watched worldwide.

By the way, they also sold merchandise together.

Malaysia: A Country Where You Never Go Hungry

Alright, a bit of casual talk to wrap up.

When it comes to Kuala Lumpur,

you absolutely never have trouble finding food haha

I'm based in the KLCC area, right in the city center,

and nearby there are several huge shopping malls

where you can find Japanese food, Thai food, Malaysian food, Dubai food, halal food -- pretty much everything.

And most of it costs less than 600 yen.

My dinner today was

fried rice from a Thai restaurant in a shopping mall food court, and it was 450 yen.

Super cheap and delicious!

I highly recommend Kuala Lumpur!!

[Day 4] Masu Photo Shoot in a Yukata?!

title_en: [Day 4] Masu Photo Shoot in a Yukata?!
excerpt_en: By Day 4, I started using Grab (taxi) more frequently to make getting around a little easier. Today I went to the Islamic Arts Museum in the morning. #Of course using Grab #Rode about 15 minutes from the guesthouse and it was about 200 yen
body_en:
By Day 4, I started using

Grab (taxi) more frequently to make getting around a little easier.

Today I went to the Islamic Arts Museum in the morning.

#Of course using Grab

#Rode about 15 minutes from the guesthouse and it was about 200 yen

#That's why I can afford to ride

This is

a museum that exhibits Islamic art and crafts by region and theme.

What makes it special is that it introduces Islamic art

not just from Malaysia but from countries around the world.

Everyone Should Visit the Islamic Arts Museum

It was my first time here, and it was amazing!

What makes it great is the quality of the exhibits.

The museum itself is spacious,

so each exhibit has plenty of breathing room

and it's incredibly easy to view.

On top of that, the Islamic art collection comes together

with a slightly fantastical atmosphere.

If you wanted to look at everything carefully,

it could easily take about 3 hours.

I had work in the afternoon,

so I spent about 2 hours going around.

By the way, you're free to photograph the exhibits,

and all labels are in Malay or English.

If you can't read English fluently,

you can just use Google Translate's camera feature and it translates instantly.

#Seriously useful

#Of course I was using it

The Downsides of Grab

As I briefly mentioned earlier,

Malaysia's ride-hailing service, "Grab,"

is very convenient but also has its downsides.

I ran into those issues today, so let me write about the drawbacks.

1. If there aren't many Grab cars in the area, you can't get matched

2. Even if you get matched, the driver sometimes doesn't come

3. If you're in a hurry, you need to book early

First, regarding 1:

I was planning to go from the Islamic Arts Museum to a station 3 km away

and tried to use Grab,

but perhaps because there weren't many Grab cars in the area,

I couldn't get matched for a while.

It can sometimes take 5-10 minutes to get matched.

Regarding 2:

Even after matching, the app shows the driver arriving in 10 minutes, and you can even see them heading toward you on the GPS, but sometimes they just drive right past you.

That happened to me today --

the driver was supposed to arrive in 5 minutes,

but when I reopened the app, it said arrival in 20 minutes.

They just drove right past me haha

So regarding 3:

if you have something planned next, you should book early.

In the end, I couldn't use Grab,

and had to walk the 3 km to the afternoon shoot location in the heat haha

#The man rejected by Grab

Masu Photo Shoot in a Yukata

Now, for today's main topic:

I did a Masu Photo shoot!

This was the first Japanese person

since I arrived in Kuala Lumpur!

Her name is Misaki.

She works in Kuala Lumpur.

She saw the FOMUS Instagram ad

and reached out to me, which led to the shoot.

And what was really nice

was that she brought along a Malaysian friend.

For this trip, I ran the FOMUS Masu Photo ad

targeting "100% women."

However, I received messages from several people who appeared to be men,

and honestly, that was a bit scary so I didn't reply.

So, I didn't have a single Malaysian male model

who I felt safe shooting with,

but thanks to Misaki,

a male model appeared!

#So grateful

And I'd heard beforehand that she had a yukata, so I asked her to wear it for the shoot.

As we went around the city doing the Masu Photo shoot,

the yukata was incredibly eye-catching!!

There's simply nobody else wearing a yukata anywhere.

Especially in Kuala Lumpur.

A Japanese person wearing traditional "kimono" must be quite rare here.

For today's shoot, four people gathered.

Me, Misaki, Misaki's Malaysian friend, and Misaki's Malaysian photographer friend.

That's right -- besides me, there was another person

who does photography as a hobby.

So,

once the Masu Photo shoot was done,

his photo time began haha

I don't often get to see how overseas photographers shoot,

so it was fun.

In particular, regarding my concern that "Masu Photo = photos taken by MaSU" had become too ingrained,

when the subject wore the "masu accessories" instead of holding the FOMUS masu,

other photographers had no problem shooting too,

so it seems like a "new form of Masu Photo"

beyond just holding the FOMUS masu for photos could be possible.

Honestly, this was exactly what I expected.

And once again, they said they wanted to buy the accessories.

It's still just a hypothesis,

but once people try on the masu accessories and take a Masu Photo,

they might end up wanting them.

*This is still just a hypothesis

If that's the case, it might be good to set up a place where people can try on the accessories.

#Targeting people from overseas

And the combination of yukata and masu accessories was perfect, so collaborating with places that offer kimono or yukata overseas is also a possibility.

So,

today was a day where I confirmed that wearing a yukata for overseas Masu Photo shoots works incredibly well.

Providing yukata,

doing the photo shoots,

and making masu accessories.

We can even make photo books.

By combining the strengths and talents

of the people involved with FOMUS, the possibilities are limitless.

I'll keep exploring!

[Day 5] Japan of Anime and Manga

excerpt_en: Day 5 of the Malaysia stay. Hard to believe we're already at the halfway point. Today I had 2 shoots planned, but 1 was postponed due to the other person's circumstances. #We managed to reschedule before I leave. So, a shoot from the morning. I received over 50 Masu Photo offers from Malaysians
body_en:
Day 5 of the Malaysia stay.

Hard to believe we're already at the halfway point.

Today I had 2 shoots planned,

but 1 was postponed due to the other person's circumstances.

#We managed to reschedule before I leave

So, a shoot from the morning.

I received over 50 Masu Photo offers from Malaysians, and out of those, I personally select the people I want to hold the FOMUS masu.

There was one Malaysian person

who does cosplay of Japanese anime characters,

and I was really intrigued,

so I did a Masu Photo shoot with her this time.

Japanese Anime and Manga Culture Is Incredible

Those of you reading this newsletter

probably already know,

but Japanese anime and manga have tons of works that are massive hits around the world.

As I wrote in an earlier article,

go to any bookstore and there's a Japanese manga section,

translated into various languages like Japanese, English, and Chinese.

The person I did the Masu Photo shoot with today

was a woman named Yeri, a 25-year-old.

On weekends, she apparently often participates in cosplay events held in Malaysia.

Looking at her Instagram,

almost all her photos are cosplay.

Login • InstagramWelcome back to Instagram. Sign in to check out what your friinstagram.com

And what's interesting is

that she can speak a little bit of broken Japanese.

When I asked how she learned Japanese,

she said she picked it up from anime and manga.

I've known for a long time that Japanese manga and anime

are popular overseas,

but I hadn't had many opportunities to actually interact with fans on the ground, so it was a really meaningful time.

For this shoot,

she came dressed as the character Akane from Oshi no Ko.

#Glad I knew about Oshi no Ko

Personally, I've been watching anime

and reading manga lately,

so I'm good at this kind of conversation.

Thanks to that, the shoot went really smoothly!

Of course, most of our conversation was in English,

but she also wanted to speak Japanese,

and was trying her best to use it.

#She said it was her first time meeting someone who came from Japan just to do a shoot

I once again felt that the influence of manga and anime is tremendous.

FOMUS also has characters called "KUKU" who wear masu.

I'd always wanted to make an anime eventually,

but now I've decided I'm definitely going to do it.

Of course, I don't know how many years it'll take.

We'd need to raise about 500 million yen.

#That's what an anime movie costs

That's how difficult anime movies are,

and we'd also need to create a story for KUKU.

I understand the importance of the screenplay,

so that will have to wait until I connect with a professional writer who's truly serious about it.

#I could connect with someone if I tried

#But the timing isn't right

#We'll build the budget steadily

KUKU are characters who wear masu,

so by expanding FOMUS's activities in multiple directions,

they'll become more discoverable.

What I can do right now

is simply get FOMUS and KUKU to as many people as possible.

That's how important

today's Masu Photo shoot felt!

By the way,

she said she'll do KUKU cosplay next time!

KUKU cosplay in Malaysia or Japan would be amazing!

#I showed her the KUKU banner too

Having content that connects people across countries

is truly powerful.

#I told her to let me know when she comes to Japan

So,

I reconfirmed that anime and manga are truly powerful.

Got in an Accident

Since this is MaSU's Malaysia activity journal,

I can write about things as they happened, which makes it interesting.

In the afternoon, I was using Grab

to head to the suburbs of Kuala Lumpur,

and guess what --

we got rear-ended on the highway.

There was a big BANG.

It wasn't at high speed,

so I wasn't injured,

but it was pretty scary.

In Japan, if you get into an accident, you call the police

and have to wait on the road,

so I was thinking,

"Ugh, I'm never going to reach my destination..."

But the driver apparently worked things out with the other driver,

and 5 minutes later, we were on our way.

"Wait, that's it?!"

I thought maybe he'd drop me off somewhere nearby after getting off the highway,

but no,

as if nothing had happened,

he drove me all the way to my destination.

Is this a cultural difference?

It was dangerous,

but I had quite a rare experience haha

Heading to Putra Mosque

The destination I reached in that accident-involved taxi

was Putra Mosque, famous for its pink design.

Putra Mosque is located in Putrajaya,

a city 20 km from Kuala Lumpur.

*In Malaysia, Putrajaya is the third federal territory after Kuala Lumpur and Labuan.

Putra Mosque

is known for its beautiful pink dome made of rose-colored granite, and attracts many tourists.

Since I came alone,

I only took selfies and Masu-Game photos,

but everyone else seemed to be having a great time taking pictures.

What's interesting is that at these kinds of mosques,

you can't enter with exposed skin.

Since Malaysia is in perpetual summer, it's incredibly hot,

and most tourists are in short sleeves and shorts.

So they lend you robes at the entrance.

Everyone puts them on for photos,

and the robes are red and very photogenic.

I thought the design of this setup was really well done.

Since I always wear all-black from head to toe,

I didn't need to borrow a robe haha

There were a few Japanese people around too.

Moroccan Pavilion

Google told me that about a 20-minute walk from Putra Mosque,

there's a place with beautiful Moorish-style architectural decorations and tiles

inspired by a Moroccan town, so I went.

It's not very big,

but it's a photogenic spot

and lots of people come to take photos.

Everyone was taking pictures in pairs or groups of three,

while I was alone with my Masu-Game, shooting away! haha

But I got some great photos thanks to that.

If you're going to Malaysia,

I really recommend going the extra distance to Putrajaya!

My round trip from the city center cost about 2,700 yen.

It would be a bit cheaper if you use the train.

A Little Secret...

Lastly, this isn't a big deal,

but my accommodation is

in a high-rise apartment building.

It's a 5-minute walk from two stations,

and honestly, the access is so good you can go pretty much anywhere.

Large shopping malls are within walking distance too.

And yet, it's only about 1,200 yen per night.

This is because a room in the apartment has been converted into a guesthouse.

While the rooms aren't private, you get the real experience of what it's like to have a room in a Malaysian apartment.

I happened to find it while looking for somewhere in downtown Kuala Lumpur with high ratings and low prices,

so it was just luck,

but if you're ever going to Kuala Lumpur

and want a cheap pseudo-experience of living in an apartment, let me know! haha

Now, I'm off to

a bar at a fancy hotel

for drinks with my friend and Misaki, who helped with the Masu Photo shoot yesterday -- three of us.

This is actually my first time drinking alcohol in Malaysia!!

I figured I wouldn't be able to write the newsletter after drinking,

so I wrote it beforehand!

See you next time!!

[Day 6] People Who Take on Challenges Are Cool

title_en: [Day 6] People Who Take on Challenges Are Cool
excerpt_en: Day 6 of the Malaysia stay. Today I had 2 Masu Photo shoots again. One was a Malaysian person. The other was a Japanese person. Both of them reached out after seeing the Instagram ad (Japanese version) I posted in early September.
body_en:
Day 6 of the Malaysia stay.

Today I had 2 Masu Photo shoots again.

One was a Malaysian person.

The other was a Japanese person.

Both of them reached out after seeing the Instagram ad (Japanese version) I posted in early September.

It's quite rare for a Malaysian person

to see a Japanese-language ad and reach out.

#Their application comment was in English

That said, looking at their Instagram photos,

they didn't seem like a bad person,

and they had a really great look

for a Masu Photo model,

so of course I said OK!

Since all our exchanges were in English,

I was pumping myself up thinking,

"This whole shoot is gonna be in English! Let's go!!"

But when we met up

and I introduced myself in English,

she responded in Japanese!!! haha

And incredibly fluent Japanese at that!!!

"What?!?"

I was pretty shocked.

Turns out, she's a professor

who teaches Japanese at the top university in Malaysia (like the equivalent of Tokyo University in Japan).

Yeah, a seriously impressive person showed up!!

No wonder she could read my Japanese ad haha

When I heard her story,

she said that since MaSU was messaging her in English,

she figured I wanted to practice my English and went along with it.

That's so kind!! haha

#Nothing but gratitude

So,

after the introduction, we switched to Japanese for everything haha

During the Masu Photo & masu accessories shoot, she told me all sorts of things, but there wasn't nearly enough time!

Originally, I had 3 shoots planned for today,

but one person contacted me the night before to say they couldn't make it.

In a way, that cancellation gave me

extra time,

so after the shoot, we went to a cafe and had a long conversation.

#Always thinking positively

Her name is Alia,

and she worked at a major Japanese company,

then switched to a Japanese venture ad agency,

and after COVID and other circumstances, returned to Malaysia,

where she now teaches Japanese as a university professor.

*At the ad agency, she was apparently in charge of the Malaysia and Thailand side of work related to the Tokyo Olympics

Malaysian universities apparently have a break from June to October,

and professors don't need to teach during that period,

so they can go all-in on side jobs.

Of course, since it's a university contract, they still receive a stable salary during the break period.

On top of that,

she also does translation work, ad agency work,

embassy work, and more,

so she's active across a wide range of fields.

Going forward, she plans to take on the challenge of getting more people to know about Malaysian culture.

That's how full of energy she was.

But she wasn't always that energetic -- during the several years she worked at a major Japanese company, she had zero room to think.

Going to work in the morning, coming home at night.

Then going to work again in the morning -- she said that the Japanese corporate structure, with its endless cycle, left no room to think about dreams or aspirations.

This was something

I could deeply relate to.

For 2 years after graduating college, I worked at a high-end hotel as a company employee, and it was the same cycle of working from morning to night, going home, and going back to work,

so I never even thought about

my own dreams or what I wanted to do.

However, in my first year,

I felt that I couldn't keep doing this job for 40 years,

and to break free from the company's conditioning,

I started going to the bookstore before work every day to read self-help books.

That's where I learned about online communities,

discovered that people younger than me in these communities were going independent, and through various twists and turns, here I am today.

#I just skipped over a lot haha

What I'm trying to say is,

even someone with a lot of drive

will struggle to be creative in a company environment where things move slowly and new things aren't embraced.

Through all these conversations,

I was able to put that into words once again.

Some people reading this newsletter

might be in their early 20s,

and unlike the old days, we have the internet now,

so you can create your own work or take on freelance jobs.

Being employed at a company

may not be the only right answer.

The Big Sister Who Moved Her Whole Family to Malaysia

Now, the second Masu Photo shoot of the day was with

Arisa, who has been in Kuala Lumpur for 7 months.

She came with her child.

She too had a life story far from ordinary,

with all sorts of experiences.

Language study abroad, working holidays, yoga retreat,

she'd been active in various countries and speaks English fluently.

She has a physical therapist license,

and she's also apparently the first person in Japan with a certain Ayurveda certification.

#This is why I can't stop doing Masu Photo -- these are the kind of people who apply

#Everyone who participates in holding a masu for a photo is a bit unusual

#FOMUS tends to attract interesting characters

#It's the best

The shoot is of course

primarily about taking Masu Photo pictures,

but hearing about local life and people's stories is also a goal, so through Masu Photo shoots, I gain so much.

#Masu Photo shoots are also a communication tool

After the shoot this time too,

we went to a nearby shopping mall and had a nice long chat.

She received an offer to be the okami (head hostess) at a high-end sushi restaurant in Kuala Lumpur, and moved her whole family here -- the epitome of someone who just goes for it.

She's exactly my type of person haha

#She also said yes to the Taiwan cycling project in one second flat

#Like attracts like

I'm in my 20s,

and I've reached a point where I'm really thinking

about how to spend the rest of my 20s.

As an address hopper,

it's been almost 2 years since I started traveling around Japan.

By making this choice, FOMUS has definitely reached many more people, and above all, I've made friends and comrades all across Japan since entering the workforce.

This is an incredibly valuable asset for me,

something that money absolutely cannot buy.

Quitting my company job

and leaving my prime Tokyo location to become a homeless address hopper --

in my mind, these are already success stories.

And so, how should I spend the rest of my 20s?

Of course, there's no reason to stop my FOMUS and photography activities, so I'll continue those,

but when it comes to my environment, there's a lot to think about.

Coming to Malaysia,

meeting people who run businesses here,

meeting people who were hired locally and are crushing it --

encountering all these different people

has helped clarify what I need right now.

One of those things is "language."

FOMUS is a brand and community

created to take on the world.

I know that having a translator would solve this issue to some extent, but being able to speak the language yourself is overwhelmingly better.

During Masu Photo shoots too, if I could speak more English,

there have been many times I've thought, "If only I could go deeper in conversation!"

I was discussing these things

with the big sister as well.

After hearing a lot from her,

what I thought was, "That actually sounds great!!" --

first doing a language study abroad for several months on a student visa,

working on improving my English,

and then making use of a working holiday.

I was already planning to use a working holiday

to go to some country next year,

but language study abroad was never on my radar.

The advantage of language study abroad is

that the people gathered there

come from various countries

and are all there to study "English."

In other words, if you become friends with people at language school,

you can use those connections in the future

when you're traveling around the world.

In my case,

I'd rarely do actual labor through a working holiday,

so for MaSU,

spending several months at an overseas language school would be much better! -- that was the advice I received.

You might be able to hear this kind of advice

in Japan too,

but I'm someone who is most moved by first-hand information,

and the words that matter most come from people who've actually experienced it and are currently working overseas based on that experience.

Doing language study abroad for several months, making friends,

and then doing a working holiday afterwards --

I still have time for that.

#Working holidays are a privilege until age 30

Wouldn't that be better for spreading FOMUS too?

That's what I'm thinking as of September 11, 2023.

I wanted to document how I'm feeling right now in an article, so I've been writing whatever comes to mind.

This is a record of my activities in Malaysia, so please bear with me.

Of course,

this is based on my own current situation,

and doesn't apply to everyone.

Even so, it might be useful for some.

The biggest advantage of FOMUS's activities,

in my opinion, is "people."

When it comes to Masu Photo,

I'm glad I used the wording

"Models wanted to hold a masu and take photos"

rather than "I'm doing shoots in Kuala Lumpur, want to be photographed?"

From what I've heard,

everyone applied because "they found the idea of holding a masu for photos interesting."

That makes me incredibly happy.

With this newsletter too,

I hope that by sharing my reality with you all,

it can lead to some kind of learning or realization.

[Day 7] The Real Kuala Lumpur Scene

excerpt_en: It's only been 7 days since I came to Kuala Lumpur, but this time, I want to share things I noticed after a week's stay based on my actual experience. This is just what I learned in one week, so please understand I won't accept any "that's wrong!" from people who've lived here for a year
body_en:
It's only been 7 days since I came to Kuala Lumpur,

but this time, I want to share things I noticed after a week's stay based on my actual experience.

This is just what I learned in one week,

so please understand I won't accept any "That's not right!" from people who've been living here for a year haha

Transportation Is Cheap!

First, let's talk about transportation.

It's overwhelmingly cheaper than Japan.

Riding one stop on the Yamanote Line costs around 160 yen, right?

In rural areas, even one stop can be 200-300 yen.

The Skyliner to Narita Airport

is about 2,500 yen one way, isn't it?

In Kuala Lumpur,

the cheapest fares are 1 RM.

That's about 30 yen.

I use the train a lot,

and each trip costs me only about 30 to 120 yen.

By the way, the express train from Kuala Lumpur International Airport to KL Sentral station in the city center takes 30 minutes and costs about 1,400 yen.

That's how cheap it is!!

And taxis in Japan

cost a fair amount even for just a few kilometers,

but here, using Grab (like Uber),

it's about 6 RM for up to 5 km.

That's about 300 yen.

When I went to the suburbs, it was about 40 RM,

which is about 1,200 yen,

but doing the same thing in Japan would cost a fortune.

For Malaysians,

Grab is somewhat expensive,

but tourists use it all the time.

This is a business in itself.

By the way, there are also

many free buses available.

*I don't ride them since they're always packed

*Pickpocketing is reportedly common too

*If my DSLR camera got stolen, it'd be game over

So that's the deal -- transportation is cheap compared to Japan!

Food Is Relatively Cheap!

Food is also cheaper compared to Japan.

Apparently, cooking at home isn't really part of Malaysian culture,

and most people eat at shopping malls or street stalls.

I've avoided eating at street stalls this time

since an upset stomach would interfere with my activities,

but cheap options run from about 100 to 300 yen.

The two shopping malls I've been frequenting this week --

"Suria KLCC" and "Pavilion" --

have incredibly extensive food courts,

where you can eat for about 300 to 800 yen.

One time I ate at a local shop and it was 200 yen.

The Water Situation

In Japan, people can drink tap water,

but overseas, tap water isn't recommended,

so you basically buy water.

Compared to Europe and Japan,

water in Malaysia is cheap.

A 500ml bottle costs about 60 to 100 yen.

Since it's always summer here, you need to stay well hydrated,

and I buy water regularly.

Accommodation

For these 6 days,

I stayed in a room in a downtown tower apartment.

If you watch the video, you'll see

it's reasonably spacious.

And it's only about 1,500 yen per night.

The reason it's so cheap is that each room has bunk beds and is set up as a 6-person room.

I only go back to the accommodation to sleep,

so anywhere was fine for me,

plus I wanted to compare cheap and expensive accommodation and share my findings in this newsletter.

By the way, tonight I'm staying at a guesthouse in the city center with a capsule-hotel-like vibe.

It's more like beds stacked 5 high rather than rooms,

but your privacy is maintained.

This is just for one night, and it's 1,200 yen.

Incredibly cheap.

Guesthouses in Japan these days

cost about 2,500 yen,

so Malaysia is relatively cheaper.

Also, tomorrow is a four-star hotel: 9,800 yen.

The last day is a five-star hotel: 19,000 yen.

Even the Ritz-Carlton in Japan,

a five-star, is about 60,000 yen per night,

so you can see the difference is massive.

By the way, Malaysia has the cheapest five-star hotels.

Plenty of Japanese Restaurants Too

In Malaysia, Japanese restaurants and convenience stores are everywhere.

There's even Nitori, AEON, Isetan, DAISO, Kinokuniya, Don Quijote, and more,

so honestly, you won't be inconvenienced at all haha

This is just my subjective view after only one week!!

Malls with Japanese real estate tenants

tend to have a lot of Japanese businesses,

and I can see why Malaysia

is ranked as the country Japanese people most want to live in.

About Airfare

Finally, for those of you who want to go to Kuala Lumpur --

how much is the airfare?

Let me share my experience.

My round trip cost 64,000 yen.

I booked an AirAsia flight.

This is a direct flight from Haneda to Kuala Lumpur.

If you use a transit in Manila,

you can apparently get here for about 40,000 yen,

but the layover was long, so I went with a direct flight this time.

A friend of mine said

booking a route through Singapore

can get the price down to about 30,000 yen.

If you want to go cheap, you can get to

Kuala Lumpur for quite a bargain.

Summary

What did you think?

This was a brief overview,

but you can see that Malaysia is a country you can enjoy at relatively low cost.

The safety in the city center is good too.

I'll keep sharing the real story as of September 12, 2023!

[Day 8] FOMUS Masu at a Kuala Lumpur Shop!

title_en: [Day 8] FOMUS Masu at a Kuala Lumpur Shop!
excerpt_en: Hey, it's MaSU! Visiting a High-End Sushi Restaurant Overseas. Day 8 of the Malaysia activities. Today I went to "Oribe," the most popular high-end sushi restaurant in Kuala Lumpur. Originally on this Kuala Lumpur expedition,
body_en:
Hey, it's MaSU!

Visiting a High-End Sushi Restaurant Overseas

Day 8 of the Malaysia activities.

Today I went to "Oribe," the most popular high-end sushi restaurant in Kuala Lumpur.

Originally on this Kuala Lumpur expedition,

I've been mainly doing Masu Photo shoots and accessories promotion.

I had no plans to

go to a sushi restaurant at all.

Why would I pay a premium

for sushi that I could eat anywhere in Japan?

Plus, it'd probably taste better in Japan anyway!

That was my thinking,

so I figured I'd never go.

But something changed that.

It was the big sister I met on Day 6 during the Masu Photo shoot,

who lives in Kuala Lumpur.

Before the shoot, she'd mentioned in our DMs

that she works as the okami (head hostess) at a high-end sushi restaurant in Kuala Lumpur.

After the shoot, we talked more,

and I developed a desire to see the high-end sushi restaurant where this amazing person works,

so I booked lunch for today, two days later, right then and there.

#My ability to act quickly is strong

I don't mean to be rude,

but it's expensive,

and I'm not super into food,

so I went with the cheapest course.

Even so, lunch was over 10,000 yen.

#Lunch in Malaysia is normally about 500 yen

Thanking the big sister for her work as okami,

getting to hear from someone running a sushi restaurant overseas,

and eating sushi on top of that -- a 10,000-yen investment felt cheap!

#Sounds a bit calculating when I write it out

I don't often go to eat non-conveyor-belt sushi by myself,

but I chose a counter seat.

Then, besides me,

a group of 6 Japanese people arrived.

According to the chef, having only Japanese people at the counter

is extremely rare.

Apparently, the majority of customers are Chinese.

At the counter, it was me alone plus the group of 6 Japanese, making 7.

We all started our meal at the same time.

But since I was the only one on the cheapest course,

my menu was different from theirs.

Thanks to that,

since my eating and talking timing was offset from theirs,

I got to have plenty of conversations with the chef.

What I found really interesting is

that the approach is completely different

from high-end sushi restaurants in Japan.

I've eaten at sushi places in Ginza and Roppongi through work,

but here, the entertainment factor was incredibly strong!

The chef would read each customer's personality and vibe

and adjust his approach accordingly.

The group

included the restaurant owner among the 6.

#I was surprised the owner was there eating

#Being able to bring friends to your own restaurant is something to aspire to

Of course, sitting right next to them,

I could hear all sorts of conversations,

and everyone was talking business.

They were all people with their own businesses.

There were 2 women --

one was a bubbly woman in her 30s,

and the other was a lady in her 50s or so.

The younger one was the type to take videos and photos of everything,

while the older lady occasionally took photos.

The chef varied how he presented the sushi

depending on who he was serving.

He's a Japanese chef,

and he tailored his service to each individual.

Of course, I could tell he was adjusting for me too.

This time, I was fortunate to have the perk

of being the okami's friend,

and they made my meal more lavish than the standard menu.

I really can't express enough gratitude.

FOMUS Masu in Action

After finishing the meal,

we decided to take some photos,

and I revealed the FOMUS masu!

Somehow, I even got to go into the kitchen for a commemorative photo.

I took a Masu Photo together with the chef.

The chef was pleased too,

and said, "This size is perfect for serving sake!"

I'd brought a few extra mini masu with me on this trip,

so I gave him about 6 of them!

Originally, I'd thought I could use them for drinking with people at the guesthouse,

but unexpectedly, out of consideration for Islamic customs,

alcohol wasn't allowed at the guesthouse,

so no evening drinking sessions happened, and I had quite a few mini masu left over.

This was a rare opportunity, so

I made the suggestion to the chef,

and he said he'd love to use them!

And so, FOMUS masu were placed

at "Sushi Oribe," a high-end sushi restaurant in Kuala Lumpur!

Connections are truly fascinating --

this all started with

deciding around August 10th to go to Malaysia,

posting a Masu Photo model call in early September,

the big sister happening to see it and sending me a DM,

doing the shoot together,

my lunch slot two days later happening to be free,

deciding to go to the sushi restaurant,

and then FOMUS masu ending up being placed there.

If you have a FOMUS masu,

and you ever visit Kuala Lumpur,

definitely check out "Sushi Oribe"!

Also, there was a fun bonus --

the other group of 6 people

were intrigued by the masu too,

and drank sake from them!

I never expected people at the restaurant to actually use the masu for drinking

right then and there, so I was surprised!

Of course, we exchanged contact information,

and I expanded my network of business connections!

#There was even someone doing business in Singapore

#We promised to connect when I visit

How just a single connection

can snowball into so much more is truly amazing,

and it once again made me glad I'm doing FOMUS.

My Conversation with the Chef

Finally, after the group left,

I got some one-on-one time with the chef.

By the way, the chef

is also active as a goodwill ambassador for promoting Japanese food.

This sushi restaurant is in its 8th year.

What left the biggest impression on me

was that "they had a perfectly shared division of roles."

He himself started the sushi restaurant

at this location in Kuala Lumpur through a certain connection,

working hand-in-hand with the owner.

The owner is a hardcore business person,

and they each naturally fell into their roles:

the owner handles all the financial stuff,

and the chef goes all-out on entertaining customers at the shop.

When both the business person and the craftsman are top-tier,

it naturally leads to high-quality content.

The owner apparently went to great lengths

to recruit this chef.

Personally,

this chef is wonderfully unconventional in a good way --

he has none of that

"Japanese sushi should be done this way" mentality,

and instead focuses on

what he can do to make customers enjoy themselves.

And at the end,

we also talked about

what "success" means.

He said that success and happiness shouldn't

be measured by how much money you've made,

but rather by how fulfilled you feel in your current life.

I could relate to that --

having money isn't the definition of success;

what matters is how enriched your life feels.

Even with all the money in the world,

without friends or good health, that's not happiness.

Having a reasonable income

and being surrounded by the best of friends -- that might be happiness.

Since everyone has different values about happiness,

rather than comparing yourself to others,

I felt it's important to think about your own happiness and well-being.

Everyone reading this article

probably has their own definition and values of happiness.

That's what makes life interesting,

and a single encounter can completely change your life.

Since coming to Kuala Lumpur,

I've had the chance to interact with many people

and have been truly stimulated.

I want to share

what I've received and felt

through this newsletter.

I hope it can be helpful in some way!

See you next time!

[Day 9] From a 1,200-Yen Guesthouse to a 5-Star Hotel: My Impressions

title_en: [Day 9] From a 1,200-Yen Guesthouse to a 5-Star Hotel: My Impressions
excerpt_en: Day 9 of the Malaysia expedition. I'm amazed at how fast time has flown during this trip. As I've written in previous articles, on a 9-night, 10-day schedule, 7 nights were at guesthouses. 2 nights at luxury hotels.
body_en:
Day 9 of the Malaysia expedition.

I'm amazed at how fast time has flown during this trip.

As I've written in previous articles,

on a 9-night, 10-day schedule,

7 nights were at guesthouses.

2 nights at luxury hotels.

I believe I mentioned that.

Today, having stayed at all the different accommodations,

I'll write down my objective impressions of each stay.

I covered the tower apartment guesthouse and the standard guesthouse in detail in the Day 7 article, so I'll keep those brief.

Tower Apartment-Style Guesthouse

This is a "tower apartment-style guesthouse" where a room in a downtown high-rise apartment has been converted into a guesthouse.

I stayed about a 10-minute walk from KLCC, the most developed area of Kuala Lumpur.

*Famous for the Twin Towers

At about 1,500 yen per night,

I stayed for 6 nights, and the guests rotated fairly often.

My room had 2 bunk beds and 2 regular beds making it a 6-person room, and other rooms had 4-person and 2-person setups,

so it could accommodate maybe 12 people max.

Honestly, if you just want a taste of tower apartment living overseas,

this is plenty.

I'd hung out at a tower apartment in Akasaka before,

and this was comparable haha

There was a decent amount of socializing too,

so I had a good time!

The one weakness was the no-alcohol policy.

That really got me.

The Standard Guesthouse

I spent one night at a guesthouse in Bukit Bintang,

a neighborhood similar to Ginza in Japan.

It wasn't quite a capsule hotel,

but the setup was close to one.

The walls were super thin.

This one was 1,200 yen per night.

If you can accept it as just a place to sleep, there's no problem at all,

and the people on either side of me were European women.

It's probably a popular budget option for backpackers.

PARKROYAL Suites

On Day 8, I stayed at

a hotel called PARKROYAL Suites.

This is a four-star hotel.

I'd booked the 9,800-yen plan (one king bed),

but the hotel upgraded me to a special room with 4 rooms, a mini kitchen, 2 king beds, 2 sinks, 2 toilets, and a spacious living area,

all for free haha

I looked it up and this room was a 20,000-yen room.

Since it was a weekday,

they probably had empty rooms!

This was genuinely amazing.

With a room like this, even 5 people could stay comfortably,

so I'd love to come with FOMUS members someday!

The breakfast also had a decent variety,

and there was a sky pool on the rooftop.

It was a very well-equipped hotel.

Traders Hotel

Today (Day 9) I'm staying at

the Traders Hotel, a five-star hotel

in the Shangri-La chain.

Looking at the price again today, it was 14,800 yen.

It comes with full service,

and I didn't feel the need to upgrade the room for my current purposes (just working), so I went with the lowest room grade.

Even so, it has a king bed

and the room is spacious, so there's zero issues.

As shown in the video,

the club lounge is only accessible with a club-lounge plan,

but the lounge attendant was kind enough to let me

take a look around!

Getting you to think "Next time, I'll book a plan with lounge access!" --

this kind of generosity is very much a five-star hotel trait.

*As an aside, when I was a hotel worker,

I worked at a fine French restaurant, and during off-hours,

I'd show the private dining rooms to walk-in guests or couples scouting for a family meeting venue,

so I understand the sentiment.

These touches really are wonderful!

Another great thing about Traders Hotel is

that there's a free shuttle buggy running between the hotel and a nearby large shopping mall.

It's about a 10-minute walk,

but if there's a free buggy, I'm riding it haha

So I used it after daytime shopping & the evening Masu Photo shoot.

Being able to allocate staff for services like this

is what you'd expect from a five-star property.

And then!!

To fully enjoy the Traders Hotel experience,

I also went to the rooftop sky bar.

#I'm writing this article at that very sky bar

There's a DJ playing club music nonstop,

and it's lively even on a weekday.

A bottle of beer costs about 900 yen,

which is overwhelmingly cheap compared to five-star hotels in Japan.

By the way, I was the only Japanese person there,

and it was mostly Europeans and Chinese.

You'd think the customers would all be couples,

but that's not the case -- there are also groups of men.

No women alone, though.

There are a few men alone, including me haha

Total Cost of 9 Nights' Accommodation

So,

having stayed at all the accommodations on this trip,

I confirmed that each has its own appeal.

Personally, if socializing is the only goal,

there's no need to stay at a hotel.

However, since Malaysia has the cheapest

five-star hotel rates,

I highly recommend it as an experience.

Now, the total cost...

- Tower apartment-style guesthouse

6 nights for 9,030 yen

1,500 yen per night.

- Standard guesthouse

1,200 yen

- PARKROYAL Suites

9,800 yen

- Traders Hotel

14,800 yen

Total: 34,800 yen

Averaging out to 3,870 yen!!

Insanely cheap!! haha

Including the airfare, 110,000 yen covers everything,

and on the last day I'll break down the expenses in detail,

but with 150,000 yen, you can enjoy a pretty luxurious trip.

Summary

You'll almost never have trouble finding accommodation

in Kuala Lumpur.

If you're looking for cheap options,

there are plenty of places in the 1,000-yen range.

This time, I actually experienced everything from a 1,000-yen guesthouse

to a five-star luxury hotel,

and I've shared my findings with you.

In Japan,

places you can stay for under 1,000 yen are becoming scarce,

so Malaysia is actually a great option!

And from a work perspective,

the time difference is only 1 hour, so meetings are barely affected.

With Europe, you'd have a 7-hour time difference,

which makes things tough, but Malaysia and similar places are easy!

I hope this is helpful in some way!

[Bonus Edition 1] 9 Consecutive Days of Masu Photo Shoots Achieved
